skill.name: azure-deploy
skill.description: Deploy validated applications and infrastructure to Azure using Azure Developer CLI, Azure CLI, Bicep, Terraform, or CI/CD pipeline recipes.
skill.summary: Run validated Azure deployments with subscription, location, environment, and post-deploy verification guidance.
skill.author: microsoft

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, filesystem, network, and credential patterns. Review shows these are mostly Markdown deployment recipes for Azure and CI/CD workflows, not hidden executable code or malicious exfiltration. The skill has legitimate medium operational risk because it guides real cloud provisioning, no-prompt deployment, credential configuration, and destructive cleanup examples that require user confirmation.

medium_findings.title: Unattended Azure Deployment Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill documents deployment commands such as azd up --no-prompt and Terraform auto-approve patterns. These are expected for an Azure deployment skill, but they can create or modify billable cloud resources if used without the prerequisite checks.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and semantically tied to real Azure provisioning. The risk is operational rather than malicious because the skill requires validation and user confirmation steps.

medium_findings.title: Remote Script Pipe to Shell
medium_findings.description: The SDK reference recommends installing azd by piping a remote script into bash. This is a known risky installation pattern because it executes remote content directly, even though the URL appears to be an official Microsoft short link.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pipe-to-shell pattern is directly present and executable. The source appears legitimate, so this is a risky practice finding rather than evidence of malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Azure Credential Variables in CI Examples
medium_findings.description: The CI/CD examples reference Azure client, tenant, and subscription secrets. This is standard for Azure federation workflows, but incorrect handling could expose deployment credentials or over-broad access.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples clearly use Azure secret variables in deployment workflows. They do not print or exfiltrate secrets, so the risk is legitimate credential exposure through misuse.

low_findings.title: Static Pattern Matches Are Mostly Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: Many static findings for Ruby backticks, weak cryptography, and path traversal come from Markdown inline code, relative links, placeholders, or Azure file path references. I found no executable source file that uses these patterns for obfuscation, exfiltration, or command injection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are Markdown guidance and examples, not runnable skill code. The skill still has deployment risk, but these specific pattern matches are largely false positives.

low_findings.title: Destructive Cleanup Example Requires Confirmation
low_findings.description: The Bicep recipe includes an Azure resource group delete example, but the global rules require ask_user before destructive actions. This remains a low residual risk because users could approve or copy the cleanup command.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The destructive command exists, but the surrounding skill policy explicitly requires confirmation. That mitigates the issue without removing operational risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: No-Prompt Cloud Provisioning
dangerous_patterns.description: The recipes include no-prompt deployment commands that can provision infrastructure and deploy application code after validation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The no-prompt deployment commands are clearly present. The surrounding checklist reduces but does not remove the risk of unintended cloud changes.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Install Script Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The azd quick reference uses a curl-to-bash install command, which executes downloaded content directly.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The dangerous command form is explicit. The domain appears official, but remote script execution remains a valid dangerous pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Destructive Azure Delete Command
dangerous_patterns.description: A cleanup section shows an Azure group deletion command with automatic yes confirmation. The global policy requires explicit user confirmation before using it.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The destructive command is present and marked destructive. The confidence is high for the pattern, while malicious intent is not supported by the context.

content.value_statement: Azure deployments fail when subscription, region, environment, or IaC steps are skipped. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through validated Azure deployment workflows with checks and verification.
actual_capabilities: Checks that an Azure deployment plan exists and is marked Validated before deployment.
Guides subscription, resource group, and region confirmation before provisioning commands run.
Provides deployment recipes for azd, Azure CLI, Bicep, Terraform, and CI/CD workflows.
Includes verification steps and troubleshooting references for common Azure deployment failures.
Documents destructive action confirmation rules for cleanup and resource deletion cases.
limitations: Does not create or prepare the application architecture before deployment.
Requires Azure credentials, tools, and permissions to exist in the user environment.
Can create billable Azure resources when deployment commands are approved and run.
Region availability guidance may need confirmation against current Azure service availability.

use_cases.title: Ship a validated app
use_cases.description: Deploy an app after preparation and validation are complete, then verify the deployed endpoint.

use_cases.title: Provision infrastructure safely
use_cases.description: Run Bicep, Terraform, or azd deployment flows with subscription and region checks.

use_cases.title: Operationalize deployments
use_cases.description: Use CI/CD examples and credential setup notes to build repeatable Azure deployment pipelines.

prompt_templates.title: Deploy with azd
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the azure-deploy skill to check my validated plan, confirm subscription and region, then guide an azd deployment.

prompt_templates.title: Deploy Bicep infrastructure
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the azure-deploy Bicep recipe to confirm prerequisites, run the required checks, and deploy my infrastructure.

prompt_templates.title: Apply Terraform safely
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the azure-deploy Terraform recipe to verify the plan, select the workspace, apply the saved plan, and collect outputs.

prompt_templates.title: Build release pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the azure-deploy CI/CD recipe to review my pipeline requirements, credentials, environments, and deployment verification steps.

output_examples.input: Deploy my validated container app to Azure.
output_examples.output: The assistant confirms the validated plan, asks the user to confirm the subscription and region, runs the appropriate deployment recipe, and reports verification results.

output_examples.input: My Static Web Apps deployment failed because the region is unavailable.
output_examples.output: The assistant identifies supported regions, recommends a compatible location, updates environment guidance, and reruns verification after deployment.

output_examples.input: Create a GitHub Actions deployment flow for my Azure app.
output_examples.output: The assistant outlines required federated credentials, repository secrets, environment variables, approvals, and post-deployment checks.

best_practices: Run this skill only after preparation and validation have completed successfully.
Confirm Azure subscription, region, and resource group impact before provisioning.
Use saved plans, environment protection, and explicit verification for production deployments.
anti_patterns: Do not run deployment commands before the validation proof is present.
Do not assume subscription, region, or resource group from local defaults.
Do not approve destructive cleanup or high-cost resources without explicit user confirmation.

faq.question: Does this skill create Azure applications?
faq.answer: No. It deploys applications after preparation and validation are complete.

faq.question: Which deployment tools does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers azd, Azure CLI, Bicep, Terraform, GitHub Actions, and Azure DevOps patterns.

faq.question: Can it run production deployments?
faq.answer: Yes, but production use should require explicit approvals, validated plans, and post-deployment verification.

faq.question: Does it handle Azure credentials?
faq.answer: It references standard Azure credential variables and CI secrets, but it does not store credentials.

faq.question: Why does it ask for subscription and region?
faq.answer: Wrong subscription or unsupported region choices can create cost, access, or deployment failures.

faq.question: Is this skill safe for beginners?
faq.answer: It is usable by beginners with guidance, but deployments can create cloud costs and should be reviewed.
